---
title: "Generalised additive mixed models: closing the loop"
---

::: {.callout-tldr title="Fast path"}
A **GAMM** has smooth terms *and* random effects in one model. But the
[GAM chapter](gam.qmd#the-hinge-a-penalty-is-a-variance) already showed that a
smooth **is** a random effect — both are a quadratic penalty block
$\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k$ on a slice of the coefficients. A GAM already fits a
*sum* of penalty blocks. So a GAMM is "a GAM with one more kind of block", needs
**no new solver**, and the single [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) loop
estimates every smoothing parameter *and* every variance component together. This
is where the ladder folds shut.
:::

Rung 5 of [the ladder](index.qmd), and the shortest — because by now you have all
the parts.

## 1. The model

Put smooths and random effects in the same predictor:

$$
g\big(\mathbb{E}[y]\big) = X\beta + \sum_k f_k(x_k) + Z b,
\qquad f_k = B_k\gamma_k,\quad b\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_b^2 I).
$$ {#eq-gamm}

A typical use: a smooth age trend ($f$) estimated while accounting for a random
intercept per scanning site ($Zb$).

## 2. Everything is a penalty block

Write the two regularised terms side by side:

| term | penalty | strength |
|---|---|---|
| smooth $f_k$ | $\lambda_k\,\gamma_k^{\top}S_k\gamma_k$ (roughness) | $\lambda_k$ |
| random effect $b$ | $\dfrac{\sigma_e^2}{\sigma_b^2}\,b^{\top}b$ (ridge) | $\sigma_e^2/\sigma_b^2$ |

They are the *same kind of object* — a quadratic penalty on a block of
coefficients, with a strength to be chosen — differing only in the matrix $S_k$
(a difference/curvature operator for a smooth, the identity for a random
intercept). The total penalty is just their sum, $\sum_k \lambda_k S_k$, slotted
into the one penalised solve we have used since the GLM:

$$
\Big(X^{\top}WX + \textstyle\sum_k \lambda_k S_k\Big)\,\gamma = X^{\top}Wz .
$$ {#eq-gamm-solve}

## 3. One loop selects them all

Because the blocks occupy **disjoint** slices of the coefficient vector, the
[Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd#the-update) update for each $\lambda_k$
decouples (the disjoint-block simplification on that page): one outer loop nudges
every smoothing parameter and every variance component at once, each from its own
block's quantities. The inner loop is still the [GLM](glm.qmd)'s penalised IRLS.
No part of this is new.

Concretely, `nitrix` exposes a random effect as just another basis-plus-penalty
through `re_smooth` (the `bs="re"` block), and `gam_fit` accepts a *list* of
smooths that can freely mix spline smooths and random-effect blocks. Factor-wise
smooths (`by_factor_smooth`) and varying-coefficient terms (`varying_coefficient_smooth`)
are the same idea with structured blocks. The [GLMM](glmm.qmd) few-level path is
*literally* `gam_fit` with a `re_smooth` block and a GLM family — a GAMM by another
name.

## 4. The ladder, folded

Stand back and the whole suite is one statement:

> **Fit $(X^{\top}WX + \sum_k \lambda_k S_k)\gamma = X^{\top}Wz$ by penalised
> IRLS, and choose the $\lambda_k$ by Fellner–Schall.**

Every rung is a choice of what goes in that sum:

- no blocks → [GLM](glm.qmd);
- identity blocks → [LME](lme.qmd) (random effects);
- roughness blocks → [GAM](gam.qmd) (smooths);
- both, non-Gaussian → [GLMM](glmm.qmd);
- both kinds of block at once → **GAMM** (here);
- a **kernel** block $S=K^{-1}$ → [Gaussian process](hsgp.qmd) (next).

The reason the suite is *one* suite, not six fitters, is this single solve plus
this single selector.

## See also

- Code: [`nitrix/stats/gam.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/gam.py)
  (the multi-block fit), [`nitrix/stats/basis.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/stats/basis.py)
  (`re_smooth`, `by_factor_smooth`, `varying_coefficient_smooth`).
- The unification it rests on: [GAM §6](gam.qmd#the-hinge-a-penalty-is-a-variance)
  and the [Fellner–Schall](fellner-schall.qmd) page.
